feedback.text: Greetings, and thank you for taking the time to consider my suggestions. I would like to propose that you include video games in the scope of the Digital Fairness Act (DFA). In 2024, the worldwide gaming market generated an estimated revenue of nearly 455 billion U.S. dollars, yet it remains largely unregulated. Extending the DFA to encompass video games would help address this significant oversight. My first suggestion is related to unfair commercial practices. Recently, thousands of games were removed from online gaming stores such as Steam and Itch.io due to actions taken by payment processors like Visa and Mastercard. These companies faced ideological pressure from an activist group, resulting in threats directed at the gaming platforms. Visa and Mastercard indicated that they would cease all dealings with these platforms unless their arbitrary demands were met. Consequently, the gaming platforms were forced to comply with these demands, which were imposed for ideological reasons and had no basis in law. The gaming platform, the game developers, and the consumers have all engaged in lawful business transactions among themselves. A third party, such as a payment processing company, should not have the authority to dictate what lawful business practices can or cannot occur. My second suggestion is related to gambling in video games. Many games incorporate elements of gambling; for instance, Overwatch features loot boxes that allow players to spend real money for a chance to receive random in-game items. This creates a risk-reward scenario similar to traditional gambling. Some developers have taken this concept further by recreating entire casino resorts within video games. A notable example is GTA 5, which includes The Diamond Casino & Resort. In this game, players can spend real money to purchase in-game currency known as Shark Cards, which can then be exchanged for chips used in various gambling activities within the casino. Alarmingly, there is currently no regulation governing this aspect of the game. To address these concerns, the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) should be extended to regulate gambling practices in video games. Additionally, Activision, a major player in the gaming industry, holds a patent(US20160005270A1) for a matchmaking system designed to influence player purchases. This system employs psychological tricks, such as pairing players with others who possess vanity items typically acquired through gambling, thereby encouraging additional spending. This evidence suggests a malicious intent to psychologically manipulate players into engaging in gambling practices, which should be stopped. My third suggestion addresses the issue of companies rendering their games non-functional after consumers have paid the full price for the product. Gaming companies continue to destroy the products they create, leaving consumers who have paid full price without access to their purchases. An example of this is Ubisoft, which developed Hyper Scape. The game was rendered non-functional as the servers were shut down, leaving players without access to the game they had purchased. The Stop Killing Games initiative seeks to safeguard the products consumers have purchased by encouraging developers to plan for the end-of-life phase of their games. This ensures that consumers who have purchased these games can continue to enjoy them even after official support has ended. I urge you to consider this initiative and explore ways to incorporate its principles into this act.
